title: The Pied Piper of Hamelin
summary: In the year 1284, the German town of Hamelin suffered a plague unlike any other.
full_text:
In the year 1284, the German town of Hamelin suffered a plague unlike any other. The rats came in waves—great armies of them pouring through every crack and crevice, scurrying across floors, nesting in walls, devouring grain and cheese, gnawing through beams. No cat could keep pace, no trap could stem the tide. The burghers of Hamelin met in desperate council, offering a fortune to anyone who could deliver them.

On the feast of Saints John and Paul, a stranger appeared at the west gate. He was tall and lean, dressed in a coat of many colors—pied, they called it—and he carried a silver pipe at his belt. None knew whence he came.

"For one thousand guilders," he declared, "I will rid Hamelin of every rat."

The burghers agreed.

The piper stepped into the street and raised his pipe to his lips. The first note was high and piercing. The second was lower, insistent. The third—and from every house, every barn, every cellar, the rats came tumbling. Great rats, small rats, brown and black and gray, a living river of fur and whiskers and gnashing teeth. They poured through the cobbled streets, following the piper down to the banks of the Weser River, and into the water they plunged, every last one drowning in the cold, swift current.

The people of Hamelin cheered. The plague was over.

But when the piper returned to collect his payment, the burghers offered him only fifty guilders. "Surely," they said, "a sorcerer such as yourself does this for love of the art." The piper's face darkened. He warned them of the danger of broken promises.

On the morning of June 26, while the adults were in church, the piper returned. He wore green like a hunter, and his face was terrible. He sounded his pipe once more—but this time the music was sweeter, stranger, filled with longing and distant laughter. The children of Hamelin heard it. They stopped their games and turned their heads. The music promised wonders—a land where streams flowed with honey, where fruit trees bent low with gold, where children never grew old. They followed the piper through the streets, one hundred and thirty of them, a silent procession.

The piper led them to the Koppenberg, a hill on the edge of town. There, a door opened in the mountainside. The piper entered, and the children followed. Only three were left behind—one lame, one deaf, one blind. The children were never seen again.

The street where the piper walked was forever after called Bungenstrasse, where no music may be played, where wedding processions silence their instruments in memory of that terrible day. The town chronicles recorded the event: "On the day of Saints John and Paul, 130 children departed, led by a piper, and were never seen again." The city hall itself bore an inscription documenting the tragedy for centuries.

But some say deep within the mountain, the children still dance—young forever—waiting for the piper's return. Others claim they emerged centuries later in Transylvania, founding a new settlement whose inhabitants all spoke a strange dialect of German. Scholars have debated the truth of the Pied Piper for generations. Was he a rat-catcher cheated of his fee? A child recruiter for Eastern European settlements? A pagan god reclaiming the children of a faithless town? No one knows. The mystery endures, and the story reminds us that promises, once broken, can never be fully mended.
moral: A promise broken brings terrible retribution.

source_references.title: The Pied Piper of Hamelin: History and Legend
source_references.author: Mark Twain

source_references.title: The Hamelin Rat-Clad Corps: A Study in Pied Piper Legend
source_references.author: Julius Pages

title: The Pig King
summary: A princess must break the enchantment of a pig-like king, learning that true beauty lies within.
full_text:
Once upon a time, in a kingdom far to the east, where the cities were built of ivory and the gardens bloomed with flowers that sang in the wind, there lived a king and queen who had been married for seven years but had no children. The queen grieved daily for the lack of an heir, and the king grew desperate, scouring the world for healers and seers. One summer afternoon, the queen fell asleep in her garden beneath a blooming rose bush, the air heavy with the scent of nectar and warm earth. Three fairies passed by and saw her sleeping form. The first fairy was moved by her beauty and granted her a wish: she would have a son who would be the most handsome and virtuous man in all the world, and no weapon could harm him.

The second fairy added that no one would ever be able to offend this child, for he would possess every virtue under the sun, and his wisdom would be as deep as the ocean. But the third fairy, who was bitter and cruel, her heart blackened by old grudges, whispered her curse: the child would be born with the skin and habits of a pig until he had taken a wife three times. The queen gave birth to a son who was, indeed, a pig in form—snout, curly tail, and all. The king and queen were horrified, but their love was stronger than their shame. They raised him as their son, dressing him in royal robes and teaching him the laws of state and the arts of diplomacy. He was a prince of nobility and kindness, though he would often wander out to the mud and wallow like a pig, nuzzling his parents and getting them dirty with an unabashed, animal love.

When the pig-prince grew to manhood, he demanded a wife. His parents despaired—who would look past the snout and the bristles to see the golden soul beneath? But the prince had seen a neighboring princess, a maiden of such grace and empathy that she could hear the sorrow in the wind. The king sent messengers with gifts of gold and silk, and despite the princess's initial revulsion, her father forced her to accept the match for the sake of a powerful alliance. On their wedding night, the princess hid a knife under her pillow, intending to kill the pig-prince while he slept, unable to endure the thought of a life bound to a beast.

But as she watched him sleep, she saw something that stopped her hand. In the soft light of the moon, the pig-skin seemed to shimmer and fade, revealing a man of incomparable beauty, his face peaceful and his spirit radiating a profound kindness. The pig-skin lay discarded on the floor, a heavy, coarse blanket of flesh. She took it and burned it in the fireplace, thinking she had freed him. When the prince awoke, he was horrified. "You have destroyed my skin!" he cried. "The spell required the skin to be shed naturally through three marriages. Now I must find another wife, for the spell can only be broken after three marriages!" He left in anger, though he gave her a ring as a token of his eternal gratitude for her compassion.

For years, the princess waited, her heart now bound to the prince. She learned the art of patience and the depth of true love, understanding that the pig-skin had been a mirror, reflecting the ugliness of those who judged by appearance. When the prince returned for the third time, he was no longer a pig, nor was he merely a man. He was a being of light and wisdom, his curse finally shattered. They were married in a ceremony that united their kingdoms in a bond of empathy and love, and they ruled with a kindness that became the hallmark of their reign, teaching their subjects that the most beautiful things in life are often hidden beneath a coarse exterior.
moral: Beauty lies within, not on the surface; patience and love break even the strongest curses.
source: Giovanni Francesco Straparola, The Facetious Nights

source_references.title: Lo cunto de li cunti overo lo trattenemiento de peccerille (The Tale of Tales)
source_references.author: Giambattista Basile
source_references.note: Neapolitan folktale collection; Pentamerone contains the original Italian version of the Pig King tale (La Pentamerone, Day 5, Story 1).

title: The Painted Skin
summary: In Taiyuan, during the Ming Dynasty, there lived a scholar named Wang. Centers on Demon,  the concepts of absolute evil, temptation, and the consequences of moral failure..
full_text:
In Taiyuan, during the Ming Dynasty, there lived a scholar named Wang. One morning, walking along the road before dawn, he came upon a young woman hurrying alone, clutching a bundle. She was about sixteen, beautiful, and clearly distressed. Wang's heart stirred with pity.

"Why are you walking alone so early?" he asked.

She told him a pitiable tale: her parents had sold her as a concubine to a wealthy family whose wife was cruelly jealous, beating her and starving her. She had fled with nowhere to go. Wang, captivated by her beauty, offered her shelter in his study.

She stayed. They became lovers. Wang kept her hidden from his wife, spending every night in the study.

But one day, Wang encountered a Taoist priest in the marketplace. The priest recoiled at the sight of him. "You are enveloped in an evil aura," the priest said. "You have met with something not of this world."

Wang dismissed him. Yet doubt crept into his heart. He returned home and found the study door locked. Climbing over the garden wall, he peered through the window.

What he saw turned his blood to ice. Where the beautiful girl should have been sat a hideous demon—green-faced, with teeth like saws. On the bed lay a human skin, perfect and lifelike, and the demon was painting it with brushes, adding color to the cheeks, life to the eyes. When it finished, it draped the skin over itself like a robe—and became the beautiful girl again.

Wang fled in terror. He found the Taoist priest and begged for deliverance. The priest gave him a duster to hang at his bedroom door. But the demon, enraged at being discovered, tore through the protection. She ripped out Wang's heart and fled.

Wang's wife, Chen, found her husband dead, his chest a gaping wound. She ran to the Taoist priest, who now took the matter seriously. He went to the market, where the demon had disguised itself as an old servant. With his wooden sword, the priest forced the demon to reveal its true form. He cut off its head, and the body dissolved into smoke, which he captured in his gourd. He rolled up the painted skin and tucked it away.

Chen begged the priest to restore her husband. The priest sent her to a madman on a dung heap. Humiliating though it was, Chen knelt before him. He laughed at her, struck her, made her swallow a loathsome pill. She endured it all.

When she returned home, she felt something rising in her throat. She coughed, and a human heart fell into her hands—still beating. She placed it in Wang's chest and bound the wound. By morning, Wang was breathing again.

The scholar had learned a terrible lesson about desire and deception. The painted skin that had appeared so beautiful had concealed only evil. But love—the patient, enduring love of his wife—had brought him back from death itself.

The Painted Skin is one of the most famous stories from Pu Songling's Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio, a collection of supernatural tales published in 1679 during the Qing Dynasty. Pu Songling was a scholar who failed the imperial examinations repeatedly, and his stories often critique the hypocrisy of the ruling class while exploring the thin line between human and demon, reality and illusion. The painted skin has become a potent symbol in Chinese culture—the beautiful mask that conceals monstrous intent. The story warns against judging by appearances, against the seduction of the surface, and reminds us that true beauty is found not in painted skin but in the loyal heart that braves humiliation and disgust to save the one it loves.
moral: Desire can be dangerous; not all beauty is safe.

source_references.title: Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio
source_references.author: Pu Songling (trans. John Minford)

title: The Panchatantra: The Lion and the Rabbit
summary: In a great forest of India, there lived a mighty lion named Dhandara. Centers on Gwyllion,  mountain folklore. Causes disorientation and getting lost - represents dangerous mountain paths..
full_text:
In a great forest of India, there lived a mighty lion named Dhandara. He was the king of beasts, with claws like swords and teeth like spears. But Dhandara was cruel—he would kill and eat one animal from the forest every single day.

The animals grew desperate. They held a meeting under the ancient banyan tree. 'We must find a way to stop this monster, said the wise old tortoise. 'But who among us is brave enough to face the lion?

A small brown rabbit stepped forward. 'I will go, he said. The other animals laughed. 'You? A tiny rabbit against the mighty lion? You will be eaten in one bite! But the rabbit was not afraid.

The next morning, the rabbit hopped to the lion's cave. Great King,' he said, I have come to offer myself as your breakfast. But first, I must tell you—there is another lion in this forest, bigger and stronger than you. He claims to be the true king of beasts.'

The lion roared with fury. Take me to this impostor at once!' The rabbit led him through the forest until they came to a deep well. He lives below,' said the rabbit, pointing down.

The lion looked into the water and saw his own reflection. He thought it was another lion staring back at him. Furious, he roared and lunged at the reflection. But there was nothing to catch—he plunged into the well and drowned.

The rabbit returned to the forest as a hero. The animals cheered: The clever rabbit has saved us all! Though he is small, his mind is mighty!' The forest was free once more.

The lesson: Brains beat brawn. Even the smallest creature can defeat the mightiest foe with cunning and wit.
moral: Cunning conquers strength.

source_references.title: The Panchatantra Reconstructed
source_references.author: Franklin Edgerton
source_references.note: Definitive critical edition of the Panchatantra; Book 1 (Mitrabheda) contains the lion-and-rabbit story.

title: Pygmalion
summary: On the island of Cyprus, where the foam of the sea first gave birth to Aphrodite, there lived a young sculptor named Pygmalion.
full_text:
On the island of Cyprus, where the foam of the sea first gave birth to Aphrodite, there lived a young sculptor named Pygmalion. He was a man of extraordinary talent, whose hands could coax living forms from lifeless stone. But he was also a man wounded by disappointment. He had seen the women of his city, the Propoetides, who sold their bodies and hearts without shame, and his soul recoiled. "Women are full of faults," he told himself. "I will have nothing to do with them." He vowed to remain celibate and devote his life entirely to his art.

Day after day, Pygmalion worked in his studio, surrounded by blocks of marble and lengths of ivory. But one morning, a different vision came to him. He took a block of snow-white ivory and began to carve not a goddess, not a monster, but the image of the perfect woman as he imagined her in his heart. His chisel moved with love before love had been named. He carved the curve of her cheek, the soft line of her jaw, the delicate arch of her brow. He shaped her hair as though it could be touched by the wind, her lips as though they could speak.

When he finished, Pygmalion stepped back and caught his breath. The statue was so lifelike that he half-expected her to blink. Her eyes seemed to hold light, her skin seemed to glow with warmth. She was more beautiful than any woman who had ever lived. And Pygmalion, who had sworn off love, fell hopelessly in love with his own creation.

He brought her gifts: smooth seashells from the shore, pebbles worn by the tide, flowers of a thousand colors, and amber drops from the trees. He dressed her in fine robes and hung jewels around her neck and rings on her fingers. He laid her on a couch spread with Tyrian purple and called her his bedfellow, resting her head on soft pillows as though she could feel them. He kissed her, and imagined that her lips grew warm. He spoke to her, and fancied that she listened. But she was ivory, and she could not answer.

The festival of Aphrodite came, the holiest day on Cyprus. Heifers with gilded horns were led to the altar, incense smoke rose in blue spirals, and the streets filled with worshippers. Pygmalion stood before the altar, afraid to speak his true desire aloud. "O gods," he whispered, "if you can grant all things, I pray for a bride who is the living likeness of my ivory maiden." He did not dare to ask for the statue itself. But golden Aphrodite, who was present at her own festival, heard the prayer beneath the words. The flames on the altar flared three times, leaping high into the air. It was a sign.

Pygmalion hurried home, his heart pounding. He climbed the steps to his chamber and found the statue on the bed where he had left her. He bent and kissed her lips. They were warm. He touched her hand. The ivory had softened. The cold hardness was gone, and in its place was the yielding warmth of living flesh. He felt a pulse beating beneath her skin. Her eyes opened, soft and shy, and she looked at him. She saw the sky above her, and the face of the man who had loved her into being.

Aphrodite herself blessed their union. Pygmalion named his bride Galatea, and they were married with all the honors due to a hero and his lady. In time, she bore him a daughter, Paphos, from whom the city of Paphos on Cyprus takes its name. And anywhere the story has been told, it reminds us that love, true and patient and believing, has the power to transform even the coldest ivory into a living heart.
moral: Devotion and love can bring dreams to life.

source_references.title: The Roman Myths of Creation and Metamorphosis
source_references.author: Neil Gleiman

title: The Princess and the Pea
summary: Once upon a time, in a kingdom of rolling green hills and turreted castles, there lived a prince who searched the world for a true princess to marry.
full_text:
Once upon a time, in a kingdom of rolling green hills and turreted castles, there lived a prince who searched the world for a true princess to marry. He traveled to kingdoms near and far, meeting dozens of royal maidens, but something was always wrong with each one. Some were too proud, others too dull. Some lacked refinement, others were too calculating. After years of fruitless searching, the prince returned home heartbroken. "There are princesses enough in the world," he told his father the king, "but how can I tell which one is real?"
One evening, a terrible storm descended upon the kingdom. Thunder rolled like drums of the gods, lightning split the darkness, and rain fell in sheets so thick that the guards could barely see the castle walls. The wind howled through the turrets, and ancient oaks bent nearly to the ground. As the storm raged, a knocking echoed through the great hall. The old king himself answered, for he could not bear to leave any traveler stranded in such weather.
There stood a young woman, drenched and shivering. Water streamed from her hair and clothing, pooling at her bare feet. Her dress was plastered to her body, her face was pale with cold, and yet there was something in her bearing — a quiet dignity even in this bedraggled state. "I am a princess," she said through chattering teeth. "I lost my way in the storm. I beg shelter for the night."
The queen, wise and practical, was not easily convinced. She smiled warmly but said nothing of her doubts. Instead, she went to the guest chamber and devised a test. She stripped the bed of all its linens, placed a single dried pea on the bare mattress, then piled twenty mattresses on top of the pea, and then twenty eider-down feather beds on top of the mattresses. On this mountain of bedding, the princess was to sleep.
In the morning, the queen asked how she had rested. "Oh, very badly!" the princess exclaimed. "I scarcely closed my eyes all night. Heaven only knows what could be in that bed, but I was lying on something so hard that I am black and blue all over!"
Now they knew she was a true princess, for only a real princess could possess such delicate sensitivity as to feel a single pea through twenty mattresses and twenty feather beds. The prince married her at once, overjoyed that he had finally found his real princess. As for the pea, it was placed in the royal museum, where it may still be seen to this day — if no one has stolen it.
moral: Real is recognized.

source_references.title: The Oxford Andersen: The Complete Fairy Tales
source_references.author: Jack Zipes (ed.)

title: Puss in Boots
summary: A clever cat in boots uses trickery and deceit to win his poor master wealth, a castle, and a princess. The cat's cunning elevates his master from poverty to royalty.
full_text:
There was once a poor miller who died and left his three sons nothing but the mill, the donkey, and the cat. The eldest took the mill, the second took the donkey, and the youngest was left with only the cat. "What am I to do with a cat?" the young man lamented. "My brothers can earn a living, but I will starve once I have eaten this cat and made a muff of his skin."
The cat, who had been listening, spoke in a clear, serious voice. "Do not trouble yourself, my good master. Give me a bag and have a pair of boots made for me so that I may scamper through the brambles. You shall see you are not so poorly off as you think."
The astonished young man did as asked. The cat pulled on the boots with great dignity, slung the bag over his shoulder, and set off. He filled the bag with bran and lay down in a rabbit warren, pretending to be dead. A foolish rabbit hopped in, and Puss drew the strings tight. He marched to the king's palace. "A gift from my noble master, the Marquis of Carabas!" he announced, presenting the rabbit. The king was delighted.
For months, Puss continued to bring gifts of game, always claiming they were from the Marquis of Carabas. The king grew to think very highly of this mysterious nobleman.
One day, Puss learned the king and his daughter were to ride along the riverbank. He instructed his master, "Go bathe in the river at the spot I show you. Leave the rest to me." As the king's carriage approached, Puss began to shout, "Help! The Marquis of Carabas is drowning!" The king recognized the cat and ordered the "Marquis" rescued. His servants brought fine clothes for the young man, who looked handsome in them. The princess fell in love instantly.
Puss ran ahead of the carriage. He came to fields where reapers worked. "The king will ask who owns these fields," he told the reapers. "If you say they belong to the Marquis of Carabas, you will be rewarded." He ran to meadows where mowers worked, then to farms, giving the same instructions. When the king asked who owned each property, the terrified workers all answered, "The Marquis of Carabas, Your Majesty."
Puss reached a magnificent castle belonging to an ogre — the wealthiest being in the land, who could change shape at will. The ogre was the true owner of all the fields and forests through which they had passed. Puss knocked at the castle gates. "I have heard you can transform into any animal," Puss said to the ogre. "A lion, for instance. Could you become a mouse? Surely even the greatest shape-shifter could not manage something so small."
"Could I not?" the ogre laughed, and transformed into a tiny mouse. Puss pounced and ate him in one bite.
When the king arrived at the castle, Puss threw open the gates. "Welcome to the castle of the Marquis of Carabas!" The king, overwhelmed by the young man's wealth and impressed by his charm, offered him his daughter's hand. The miller's son married the princess and became a great lord. And Puss in Boots lived out his days as a noble cat, catching mice only for sport, having elevated his master — and himself — far beyond their humble beginnings.
moral: Cleverness and presentation matter more than wealth - a cat in boots can make his master a king.

source_references.title: Histoires ou contes du temps passé
source_references.author: Charles Perrault

source_references.title: The Classical Fairy Tale: A Norton Anthology
source_references.author: Jack Zipes

title: Paul Bunyan
summary: In the early days of America, when the continent was covered with vast, untamed forests stretching from the Atlantic to the Pacific, there lived a lumberjack so enormous, so strong, and so skilled that he became the greatest hero the North Woods have ever known.
full_text:
In the early days of America, when the continent was covered with vast, untamed forests stretching from the Atlantic to the Pacific, there lived a lumberjack so enormous, so strong, and so skilled that he became the greatest hero the North Woods have ever known. His name was Paul Bunyan, and he was the king of the loggers, a giant among men, the inventor of the logging industry, and the creator of some of America's most famous landmarks. His feats are tall tales, of course, told around campfires by lumberjacks who loved to spin a good yarn, but they have become part of the very fabric of American folklore.

Paul Bunyan was born in the state of Maine, and from the very beginning, it was clear that he was no ordinary child. He was so large at birth that it took five giant storks to deliver him to his parents. When he was just a few weeks old, he could already lift a full-grown ox. As a baby, he was so restless that his parents had to float his cradle in the ocean off the coast of Maine so that the waves would rock him to sleep. One day, Paul started bouncing in his cradle, and his bouncing caused a seventy-foot tidal wave that washed away entire villages. His parents, realizing that the boy was too powerful for civilized life, moved deep into the Maine woods.

Paul grew quickly, eating fourteen cows' worth of milk each day. By the time he was a young man, he stood many feet tall, his beard was thick as a forest, and his appetite was legendary. He could clear an entire forest with a single swing of his ax. He was so fast that he once blew out a candle and jumped into bed before the room went dark. He was so strong that when he found a logging road too crooked for his liking, he simply tied one end of the road to a tree stump, hitched the other end to his ox, and pulled until the road straightened out.

It was during the Winter of the Blue Snow that Paul found his faithful companion, Babe the Blue Ox. One morning, after a night of strange blue snowfall that had painted the entire forest in shades of indigo, Paul went out to gather firewood and saw two ears sticking out of a snowdrift. He dug out what turned out to be a baby ox, frozen and near death. Paul carried it home and warmed it by the fire. The ox's hair remained blue—the color of the legendary snow—and Paul named him Babe. Babe grew at an astonishing rate. Every time Paul looked at him, he seemed to have grown two feet taller. Within a year, Babe was the size of a small house. His horns measured forty-two ax handles and a plug of chewing tobacco across. He could pull anything that had two ends. Paul and Babe became inseparable.

Together, Paul and Babe traveled across America, logging their way from Maine to the Pacific. Along the way, they created many of the nation's most famous landmarks. When Babe got thirsty, Paul dug out the Great Lakes to provide drinking water. When Babe got hungry, Paul cleared the forests of North and South Dakota, turning them into farmland. The Mississippi River was created when a water tank that Babe was pulling sprung a leak, and the water that spilled out carved a channel across the continent. The Grand Canyon was formed when Paul dragged his ax behind him while walking through Arizona. The Rocky Mountains were piled up from the rocks that Paul cleared from his logging sites. The Black Hills of South Dakota are said to be Babe's burial mound.

Paul's logging camp was as oversized as everything else in his life. The cook stove covered an acre. The hotcake griddle was so large that it had to be greased by men wearing sides of bacon on their feet, skating across the surface. Paul's crew included a cast of legendary characters: Ole the Big Swede, the blacksmith who could forge an anchor chain in a single day; Johnny Inkslinger, the bookkeeper who could write with seven pens at once; and Brimstone Bill, the only man who could handle Babe the Blue Ox.

Paul Bunyan's stories were first written down by W.B. Laughead in 1922 for a lumber company advertising pamphlet, but they had been told orally by lumberjacks for generations before that. The tales celebrate the American spirit of expansion, the courage of the frontier, and the humor of people who worked hard and played hard. Paul Bunyan may never have existed in reality, but in the hearts and imaginations of Americans, he is as real as the lakes and mountains he was said to create. He is the giant who cleared the way for a nation, the lumberjack who could do anything, the hero whose stories grow taller with every telling, just as Babe the Blue Ox grew larger with every look.
moral: Big dreams make America.

source_references.title: Paul Bunyan
source_references.author: Estyn Evans
source_references.publisher: In Fables and Folk Tales, Oxford University Press

title: Pele's Tears
summary: In the heart of the Pacific, where the earth breathes fire and the ocean churns against volcanic shores, the goddess Pele reigns as both creator and destroyer.
full_text:
In the heart of the Pacific, where the earth breathes fire and the ocean churns against volcanic shores, the goddess Pele reigns as both creator and destroyer. She is Pelehonuamea, she who shapes the sacred land, her home the Halema'uma'u crater at the summit of Kīlauea on the Big Island of Hawai'i. Her story begins in the ancient land of Kahiki, where she was born to Kūwahailo and Haumea, a family of elemental forces. From birth, Pele's fiery spirit was evident, and her eldest sister Nāmakaokaha'i, goddess of the sea, grew jealous of her beauty and power. When Pele's uncle Lonomakua gave her a magic digging stick named Pāoa and taught her to control volcanic fires, she accidentally burned the sacred lands of their home to the ground. Nāmaka's fury drove Pele to flee across the ocean in a great canoe named Honua-i-a-kea, carrying her youngest sister Hiʻiaka in the form of an egg.

Pele journeyed through the Hawaiian islands, striking the earth with Pāoa to create fire pits. At each island—Kaua'i, O'ahu, Moloka'i, Maui—Nāmaka pursued her, extinguishing the flames with tidal waves. On the western slopes of Haleakalā, the sisters fought a terrible battle. Nāmaka tore Pele's body apart, scattering her bones along the coast of Kahikinui, where they remain as masses of broken lava called Nā iwi o Pele. But Pele's spirit escaped to the island of Hawai'i, where she found her permanent home in the fire pit of Halema'uma'u.

Yet for all her power, Pele is not without sorrow. The goddess loved deeply and lost greatly. Her most famous love was Lohi'au, a mortal chief of Kaua'i. She sent her sister Hiʻiaka to retrieve him, but when Hiʻiaka's journey took too long, Pele's jealousy consumed her. In a rage, she destroyed Hiʻiaka's beloved forests and covered her friend Hōpoe with lava. When Hiʻiaka finally arrived with Lohi'au, Pele's fury erupted—she killed Lohi'au with fire. Hiʻiaka, heartbroken and vengeful, stomped through the layers of Kīlauea down to the tenth stratum, threatening to flood the crater with groundwater and extinguish Pele's fires forever. Only at the last moment did the sisters reconcile.

But Pele's sorrow did not end there. In other versions of the legend, Pele's jealousy over a lover or her grief for lost loved ones caused her to weep. As she wept, the great goddess's tears fell upon the volcanic landscape. The molten lava caught her tears mid-air, cooling them instantly into small, glassy, jet-black droplets—nā waimaka o Pele, Pele's tears. These obsidian teardrops are found scattered across Hawaii's volcanic slopes, often entangled in strands of Pele's hair, the fine volcanic glass formed when lava fountains spray molten material into the wind.

Each glistening black droplet carries the weight of the goddess's anguish. When volcanologists study these tears, they find trapped within them bubbles of gas—vesicles—that reveal secrets of the earth's interior. But to the Hawaiian people, Pele's tears are more than geological specimens. They are a tangible connection to the goddess who shaped these islands, a reminder that even the most powerful forces of nature are not immune to sorrow. Pele's tears teach that creation and destruction are two sides of the same coin, that grief and power can coexist, and that the land itself remembers the emotions of the divine.
moral: Creation weeps.

title: Pele and the Lava
summary: Across the vast, sapphire expanse of the Pacific, where the horizon dissolves into a haze of gold and blue, lies the archipelago of Hawaiʻi—a chain of emerald peaks born from the violent passion of a single deity.
full_text:
Across the vast, sapphire expanse of the Pacific, where the horizon dissolves into a haze of gold and blue, lies the archipelago of Hawaiʻi—a chain of emerald peaks born from the violent passion of a single deity. This is the realm of Pele, the goddess of fire, lightning, wind, and volcanoes. She is not a goddess of gentle warmth, but of creative destruction, a force of nature as unpredictable as the magma that flows from the heart of the earth.

Pele did not originate in Hawaiʻi. She came from the distant lands of Tahiti, sailing in a great canoe carved from the wood of sacred trees. She fled her home, driven by a fierce desire for a place where she could build her fires without restraint, and where her power would not be contested. As she navigated the endless ocean, guided by the stars and the songs of the wind, she sought a peak high enough to reach the heavens, a mountain that would serve as her throne.

When she first reached the islands, Pele found the land cold and the peaks shrouded in snow. She attempted to make her home in the Koʻolaus, but the soil was too damp, the rains too frequent. She moved to Mauna Kea, but the summit was a wasteland of ice and wind. Frustrated and longing for the heat of the deep earth, she traveled south to the island of Hawaiʻi, where she discovered Kīlauea—a mountain that pulsed with a hidden, subterranean energy. Here, the earth was thin, and the fire was waiting.

With the pau, her sacred digging spade given to her by her grandmother, Pele began to carve her kingdom. She dug deep into the crust of the world, releasing the trapped magma in a torrent of liquid gold. The fire roared, the sky turned the color of a bruised plum, and the lava flowed down the slopes in rivers of molten stone. Where the lava met the crashing waves of the Pacific, it hissed and screamed, hardening into black basalt and creating new land. Again and again, Pele expanded her shores, adding layer upon layer of stone until the islands grew and flourished.

But Pele's existence was marked by an eternal conflict. Her elder sister, Namaka, the goddess of the sea, viewed Pele's fire as an intrusion, a scar upon the pristine blue of the ocean. Namaka unleashed the full fury of the tides, sending colossal waves to drown the fires and extinguish the goddess's ambition. The battle between fire and water lasted for eons—a cosmic war of steam and ash. Pele retreated further into the mountains, deeper into the craters, but her fire never truly died. It only grew more concentrated, more potent.

Today, Pele still resides in the Halemaʻumaʻu crater, her presence felt in every tremor of the earth and every plume of sulfuric smoke. She is the goddess of the cycle of life: she destroys the forests and the villages with her lava, but in doing so, she creates the most fertile soil on Earth. The blackened lava fields eventually give way to lush greens and vibrant flowers, a testament to the fact that beauty can only be born from the ashes of destruction.

Those who venture onto her slopes do so with a mixture of awe and terror. They leave offerings of ohelo berries and lei, praying for her favor and begging for her restraint. For to know Pele is to know that nature does not negotiate. She is the flame that consumes and the fire that creates, the eternal heartbeat of the islands, reminding all who dwell there that they live at the mercy of a goddess who breathes fire.
moral: Fierce passion can create kingdoms.

title: Phoenix
summary: In the burning sands of Arabia, where the sun beats down like a hammer on an anvil and the heat shimmers in waves across the desert, there lives a bird unlike any other in creation.
full_text:
In the burning sands of Arabia, where the sun beats down like a hammer on an anvil and the heat shimmers in waves across the desert, there lives a bird unlike any other in creation. It is the Phoenix, the fire-born, the eternal one, and its story is the oldest and most beautiful of all the myths of birds.

The Phoenix was first described by the Greek historian Herodotus in the 5th century BCE, who heard of it from the priests of Egypt. He wrote that it was a bird of incredible size and beauty, with feathers of gold and crimson, a bird that lived for five hundred years and then, feeling death approach, built a pyre of fragrant woods and spices and set itself ablaze. From the ashes, a new Phoenix would emerge, carrying the remains of its parent to the temple of the Sun at Heliopolis, there to be buried.

But the story of the Phoenix is far older than Herodotus. In Egyptian mythology, the bennu bird was associated with the sun god Ra and the city of Heliopolis. The bennu was said to have been the first living thing to emerge from the primeval waters of chaos, landing on the first dry ground—the Benben stone—and letting out a cry that defined the nature of creation. The bennu was not consumed by fire but was itself a symbol of the eternal cycle of the sun, dying each evening and being reborn each morning.

It was the Romans who gave the Phoenix its most enduring form. The poet Ovid, in his Metamorphoses, described the bird in exquisite detail: "There is a bird that renews itself, the Phoenix. It does not feed on seeds or grass, but on drops of frankincense and the juices of the amomum. When it has lived five hundred years, it builds a nest of oak and palm and spices, and on this nest it dies. From its body, a new Phoenix is born, which lives until it too has grown strong enough to carry the nest to the city of the Sun, where it places it on the altar of the temple."

The Phoenix became one of the most powerful symbols in human culture. Early Christians adopted it as a symbol of the resurrection of Christ, and images of the Phoenix appear in catacombs and on early Christian tombs. The Roman emperor Elagabalus tried to serve Phoenix at a banquet, though the bird he served was almost certainly an elaborate fraud. In Jewish tradition, the Phoenix was said to be the only creature that did not eat from the Tree of Knowledge in the Garden of Eden, and thus it was granted immortality.

The details of the Phoenix's life vary across cultures, but the core story remains remarkably consistent. The Phoenix is a bird of unparalleled beauty, with feathers of gold, red, and purple. Its eyes are like twin stars, and its voice carries the harmony of the spheres. It dwells in the far reaches of Arabia or India, in a land of perpetual spring, where it drinks only from the purest springs and feeds on the rarest of spices. As its five-hundred-year life draws to a close, the Phoenix feels the call of death. It builds a nest of cinnamon, myrrh, and frankincense, the most precious of aromatic substances. It climbs onto this pyre, turns its face to the sun, and waits. The sun's rays, concentrated by some mysterious property of the bird's feathers, ignite the nest. The Phoenix is consumed, but in the heat and the smoke and the ashes, a miracle occurs.

From the golden ashes, a tiny worm emerges, no larger than a caterpillar. As the sun warms it, it grows, its skin hardening, its shape changing. On the third day, a new Phoenix rises from the pyre, its feathers even more brilliant than those of its parent. It gathers the ashes of its predecessor and carries them to Heliopolis, the city of the Sun in Egypt. There, on the altar of the great temple, it deposits the remains, and then it flies back to Arabia to begin the cycle anew.

The Phoenix represents the eternal cycle of death and rebirth, destruction and renewal. It is a symbol of hope in the face of despair, of the promise that even in the most complete and total destruction, new life can emerge. The fires that consume the Phoenix do not annihilate it—they transform it, purify it, and prepare it for its next incarnation. In this, the Phoenix embodies one of humanity's deepest and most enduring hopes: that death is not the end, that something of us survives the flames, and that from the ashes of our mortal lives, something new and beautiful can rise. The Phoenix is not merely a bird. It is the promise of eternity, incarnate in feathers and flame.
moral: Death is not the end.

source_references.title: The Book of Mythical Birds and Legendary Beasts
source_references.author: John and Caitlín Matthews

title: Púca
summary: On the night of Samhain, when the veil between worlds is thinnest and the dead walk among the living, there are tricks to be wary of.
full_text:
On the night of Samhain, when the veil between worlds is thinnest and the dead walk among the living, there are tricks to be wary of. The most famous is the Púca—the shapeshifting spirit that appears most often as a horse, a great black horse with no rider, running wild across the fields. But this is only one form. The Púca can appear as a goat, a rabbit, or even a handsome young man with knowing eyes.

The Púca is not evil, the wise ones say. It is a spirit of wild nature—the essence of the untamed earth. It lives in the hills and the forests, in the wind and the waves. It belongs to no one and serves no one. But on Samhain night, it is abroad, testing travelers, playing its tricks.

If you catch the Púca—if you can mount its wild back—it will carry you faster than any mortal horse, leaping over hedges and walls, flying across the landscape. But where it takes you, no one knows. Some say it carries you to the Otherworld, to the fairy hills, where you feast in golden halls for what seems like minutes. When it returns you to your starting place, you will not remember anything but a dream.

Others are not so lucky. There are tales of men who rode the Púca and were found days later, far from home, their minds broken, speaking of things no mortal should see. The Púca, wild spirit that it is, does not care whether you survive intact. It is testing nature, and nature is not always kind.

To protect oneself, the wise avoid the open fields on Samhain. They stay inside, with iron about—horseshoes over the door, or a nail in the pocket. The Púca cannot cross iron, they say. And those who must travel carry a piece of bread, which they throw at the first sign of the spirit—bread keeps the wild things at bay.

This is why we have Samhain: to see the Otherworld, yes, but also to respect it. And why we hide on the darkest nights—not because we are afraid, but because we know some rides are one-way.
moral: Nature is wild.

title: The Patasola
summary: A one-legged forest creature lures men into the jungle with the voice of their loved ones.
full_text:
Deep in the jungles of Colombia, where the canopy is so thick that the sun becomes a green twilight and the calls of howler monkeys echo like rolling thunder, there is a creature that every hunter fears. She is the Patasola — the One-Footed Woman — a spirit of the forest who preys on those who stray too far from the path.

The Patasola appears as a beautiful woman, her skin dark as the earth after rain, her hair long and black as a jaguar's coat. She wears a dress of green leaves and carries flowers that smell sweeter than any blossom on earth. But she has only one leg, ending in a foot shaped like a hoof, which she hides beneath her flowing skirts.

She waits beside forest trails, where the trees are thickest and the air is heavy with humidity. When a man passes alone, she calls out in a voice like running water: "Señor, I am lost. Please help me find my way home." The man, enchanted by her beauty, approaches. She lets him carry her on his back. But as they travel, her single leg wraps around his waist with crushing strength, and her fingers lengthen into claws.

There are two versions of the Patasola's fate. In one, she was a beautiful woman who was betrayed by her husband and left to die in the jungle. Her spirit became a vengeful creature that punishes all men for her husband's sin. In the other, she was a forest spirit who guarded the animals from overhunting. She targets those who kill more than they need.

The only defense against the Patasola is to stay on the main path, never travel alone, and if you hear a woman calling in the jungle, do not look back. Run in the opposite direction. The Patasola cannot follow beyond the forest's edge. She is a warning about the dangers of the wilderness and the importance of respecting the boundaries between human and spirit worlds.
moral: Not all beautiful things bring good intentions; trust only what you can verify.

source_references.title: Cultura popular colombiana: mitos, leyendas y tradiciones
source_references.author: Libia Raquel Villamizar de la Torre

title: Pichal Peri: The Backward-Footed Spirit of Pakistan
summary: A vengeful female spirit with backward-facing feet lures men to their doom in the Punjab countryside.
full_text:
In the villages of Punjab and Sindh, where the wheat fields stretch to the horizon and the canals glitter like silver ribbons under the moon, there is a creature that every traveler fears. She is the Pichal Peri, also called Churail — the ghost of a woman wronged in life, returning to take her revenge on the living.

The Pichal Peri was once a woman. She may have been a bride who died on her wedding night, a mother who perished in childbirth, or a wife driven to suicide by an abusive husband. Death did not bring her peace. Her soul, twisted by injustice, refuses to leave the world. She haunts the crossroads, the canal banks, and the abandoned wells that dot the Punjabi countryside.

She appears as a woman of breathtaking beauty. Her hair is long and black, her skin is fair, her eyes are large and dark. She wears a red bridal dress and bangles that jingle with every step. To any man walking alone at night, she seems like a vision of paradise.

But if he looks closely, he will see the truth: her feet face backward. Her footprints point in the opposite direction from where she walks. This is the sign of the Pichal Peri, the mark of the creature that is not human.

She calls to the traveler in a voice soft as silk. "Brother, I am lost. Will you help me find my way?" If the man approaches, she wraps her arms around him. Her embrace feels like heaven for a moment — then she drains his life force, leaving him a hollow husk by the roadside.

In some versions of the legend, the Pichal Peri is not entirely evil. She is searching for the man who wronged her in life, and she mistakes innocent travelers for her abuser. If a man can recite verses from the Quran or call on the name of Allah, she will flee, her shriek echoing across the fields.

Protection against the Pichal Peri includes carrying a steel knife, reciting prayers, and never walking alone at night. But the safest strategy is to avoid the canals and crossroads after sunset. In Pakistan, every village has its story of a man who met the Pichal Peri and never came home.
moral: Injustice in life creates suffering that echoes beyond death.

title: Popobawa: The Bat-Wing Terror of Zanzibar
summary: A shape-shifting evil spirit with bat wings terrorizes the island of Zanzibar, attacking victims in their sleep.
full_text:
On the spice island of Zanzibar, where the turquoise waters of the Indian Ocean lap against white sand beaches and the scent of cloves fills the air, there is a terror that wakes people in the dead of night. Popobawa is a shape-shifting evil spirit whose name means "bat wing" in Swahili. He is one of the most feared supernatural beings in East Africa.

Popobawa is described differently by different victims. Some say he is a giant bat with glowing red eyes and leathery wings. Others say he is a human-like figure that casts a shadow so dark it seems to absorb light. Most agree on one thing: he attacks at night, entering locked rooms through closed windows, and he sexually assaults his victims while they sleep.

The first wave of Popobawa panic began on Pemba Island in the 1970s. A sheikh who had been wrongfully imprisoned released a shetani — an evil jinn — as an act of revenge. The jinn became Popobawa. For years, he terrorized Pemba, then spread to Zanzibar, and eventually to mainland Tanzania.

His attacks cause mass hysteria. In 1995, during Ramadan, panic about Popobawa swept across Zanzibar. Hundreds of people reported attacks. Families slept outside in groups, believing safety lay in numbers. People placed knives under their pillows, painted their doors with Quranic verses, and refused to sleep alone.

The victims of Popobawa describe a strange phenomenon: sleep paralysis. They wake in the night, unable to move, and see a dark shape hovering over them. They smell sulfur. They hear a voice that speaks in a language they do not understand. Then the attack begins. In the morning, they wake exhausted, covered in scratches and bruises.

Some say Popobawa is a psychological phenomenon — a manifestation of collective trauma and anxiety. Others insist he is a real spirit, a jinn that feeds on fear. The attacks seem to follow a pattern: they intensify during periods of social stress, then fade for years before returning.

Popobawa is a uniquely modern legend, one that adapts to the fears of each generation. In a region where traditional beliefs in spirits coexist with modern life, Popobawa is the nightmare that bridges both worlds — the ancient fear of the dark given a name, a face, and bat wings.
moral: Fear itself is the most contagious and dangerous spirit of all.

source_references.title: Swahili Origins: Culture and Society in a Former European Colony
source_references.author: de Vere Allen, James

title: Pontianak: The Vampire Ghost of Malaysia
summary: The vengeful spirit of a woman who died in childbirth hunts men and babies in the darkness.
full_text:
In Malaysia and Indonesia, no ghost is more feared than the Pontianak. She is the spirit of a woman who died during childbirth, her soul twisted by rage and sorrow into a creature that hunts the living. Her cry is said to be the most terrifying sound in the world.

During the day, the Pontianak appears as a beautiful woman with long black hair and a flowing white dress. She can seem normal, even kind. But at night, her true nature emerges. Her fingernails lengthen into claws, her teeth become fangs, and her beauty becomes a mask for unspeakable hunger.

The Pontianak hunts in specific ways. She is attracted to the scent of pregnant women and newborn babies. She perches in trees near houses, waiting for the moment when a mother is alone. She makes a sound like a baby crying to lure people outside. If the cry is soft, she is far away. If the cry is loud, she is close. The most terrifying version of the legend says that if you hear a Pontianak's cry and it sounds right next to you, it is already too late.

To protect against the Pontianak, Malaysians drive a nail into the ground above the grave of any woman who dies during pregnancy. This is believed to pin her spirit down and prevent her from rising. Doors and windows are locked at dusk. A pair of scissors opened in the shape of a cross is placed above the baby's cot.

There is a story from the state of Johor about a man named Ahmad who heard a baby crying outside his house at midnight. His wife had just given birth. He went outside to check and found a woman in white standing under the rambutan tree. She smiled at him, and her smile was so beautiful that he forgot his fear. He took a step toward her. His wife, watching from the window, screamed. Ahmad stopped. He looked at the woman's hands and saw the claws. He ran inside and locked the door. The Pontianak screamed in fury — a sound like metal scraping metal — and vanished.

The Pontianak represents one of the deepest fears in Malay culture: the danger of childbirth, the vulnerability of infants, and the terror of a woman wronged. She is a warning to men who deceive women, to husbands who abandon their wives, and to anyone who takes love for granted. In the darkness of the Malaysian night, the cry of the Pontianak is a reminder that some wounds never heal.
moral: The wronged dead do not rest; their pain echoes through the world of the living.

source_references.title: The Malays: A Cultural History
source_references.author: R.O. Winstedt
source_references.note: Surveys Malaysian supernatural traditions including the pontianak (vampire ghost) in Malay cultural imagination.

title: Papa Bois: The Old Man of the Forest
summary: A hairy old man with cloven hooves protects the forests and punishes hunters who kill for sport.
full_text:
In the forests of Trinidad, where the scarlet ibis paint the Caroni Swamp in shades of red and the howler monkeys call across the valleys, there lives the oldest and wisest creature on the island. He is Papa Bois, the Old Man of the Forest, and he has been protecting the trees and animals of Trinidad since before the first humans arrived.

Papa Bois looks like an old man, but he is not human. His body is covered in coarse hair, his feet are cloven hooves like a goat's, and leaves and moss grow from his beard. His eyes are the color of forest shadows, and his voice sounds like the rustling of leaves in the wind. He carries a hollowed cow horn, which he uses to summon the animals of the forest or to warn them of danger.

Papa Bois is the guardian of all living things in the forest. He punishes hunters who kill for sport, leaving them lost in the woods until they learn their lesson. He protects the trees from loggers who cut without replanting. He guides lost children back to their villages. He is the conscience of the forest, the force that ensures the balance of nature is maintained.

One of the most famous stories about Papa Bois tells of a hunter who killed a deer for its antlers alone, leaving the meat to rot. As the hunter walked home, a deer appeared on the path ahead of him. He raised his gun and fired, but the deer did not fall. It turned and looked at him with human eyes. The deer spoke: "You killed my brother for his horns. Now you will walk with me until you understand the value of life." The hunter disappeared into the forest and was never seen again.

Hunters in Trinidad know to greet Papa Bois before entering the forest. They say, "Bon jour, vieux Papa," tipping their hats to the trees. If they do not, Papa Bois will make their hunt unsuccessful — spooking the game, tangling their lines, or leading them in circles until dusk.

Papa Bois is a figure of Afro-Caribbean folklore, descended from the forest spirits of West Africa but adapted to the Trinidadian environment. He represents the ecological wisdom that has been passed down through generations. In a country where deforestation and development threaten the natural environment, Papa Bois is a reminder that the forest is a living entity that deserves protection.
moral: The forest is a living entity that deserves protection; take only what you need.

source_references.title: Voodoo in Haiti
source_references.author: Alfred Métraux
source_references.note: Classic ethnographic study of Caribbean spiritual traditions including forest spirits and shape-shifting entities like Papa Bois.

title: The Princess and the Glass Mountain
summary: A princess sits atop a treacherous glass mountain, and the man who can ride to the top wins her hand — a poor boy with a magic horse succeeds where knights fail. The princess is a relative of Brunhilde of Germanic heroic legend, and the tale is very popular across the North.
full_text:
THE PRINCESS AND THE GLASS MOUNTAIN
      XVII
moral: Humility and a pure heart can achieve what pride and arrogance cannot.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XVI: The Princess and the Glass Mountain. Source: Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, p.390 (somewhat abridged). Very popular throughout the North; the princess is a relative of Brunhilde of heroic legend.

title: The Poor Devil
summary: When a peasant prays for his cow's protection, God and the Devil argue over who truly controls fate — leading to a wager that reveals the Devil's limitations and God's grace. This tale from Småland is at once humorous and philosophical.
full_text:
THE POOR DEVIL
      XXII
moral: Even the Devil has limits; divine protection is stronger than evil schemes.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXI: The Poor Devil. Source: Bondeson, p.212 (from Småland). A humorous and philosophical tale showing the Devil attempting to rival God.

title: The Plague
summary: In Swedish folk tradition, the devastating plagues were personified as a boy with a rake and a girl with a broom, wandering from village to village. Where the boy raked, some were spared; where the girl swept, death left an empty house. On Soller Island, the plague spirits strewed gold along the roads, but the treasure was so infected that anyone who touched it died before the next sunset.
full_text: Memories of the devastating epidemics that ravaged Sweden still live in folk tradition, transformed into myth over time. During the plague, a boy and a girl were seen wandering from village to village — the boy carrying a rake, the girl a broom. Where the boy used his rake, some were spared from death; but where the girl swept with her broom, death left an empty house. Places neither approached escaped entirely. On Soller Island in Lake Siljan, the plague spirits strewed gold and precious stones along the roads and paths, but the treasure was so infected that anyone who so much as moved a stone with their hand became a corpse before the next sunset. Only two wise old men, Bengh and Harold, survived on the island by resisting the lure of the gold. Many islanders fled northward through the 'Twelve-Mile Roads' toward Vermland. Among the refugees was a young maiden named Malin, who spotted a glittering jewel depicting Christ on the cross. Ignoring her companions' warnings, she picked it up. When they reached their first campsite at Rossberg, Malin fell to her knees in prayer. As the evening sun set behind the mountain, she sank lifeless upon a stone. That stone is still called 'Malin's Church,' and every midsummer the local herdsmen decorate it with fresh leaves and flowers in her memory.
moral: Those who resist the lure of easy riches may survive when others perish.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1890), Dalarne section.

source_references.title: Legends of the Plague in Scandinavian Folklore
source_references.author: Christiansen, R.T.

title: The Parson and the Clerk
summary: A miserly parson who refuses charity disguises himself as a tramp to turn away beggars. The clever clerk, also in disguise, torments the parson all night by pretending his corn has been spilled and his cattle let loose, while impersonating the parson to his wife. The terrified parson reforms and becomes generous.
full_text: A miserly parson dressed as a tramp and sat in his kitchen to turn away beggars. The clerk, also disguised as a tramp, came seeking shelter. The wife sent him to the clerk's house, but the clerk claimed it was full. He insisted on staying and was put to sleep in the brew-house with the other 'tramp' (the parson). The clerk took the bed, making the parson sleep on the bench. He repeatedly snuck out, then woke the parson claiming he had: 1) made a hole in the wood-shed loft so corn spilled among the firewood, 2) let out all the cattle. Each time the parson rushed out to fix it. Meanwhile the clerk went to the house, imitated the parson's voice, and rested by the fire with the wife. Finally the clerk claimed he had put ditchwater in the beer barrel. The parson, exhausted and reformed, ordered the barrel sent to the clerk. The parson became a generous man who never refused the poor.
moral: Stinginess brings more trouble than generosity; a night of misery can cure a lifetime of greed.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

source_references.author: Andersen, J. (ed. & trans.)

title: The Princess on the Glass Hill
summary: A determined young man seeks to win the hand of a princess who resides atop an impenetrable glass hill. Through his bravery, wit, and the help of magical entities, he overcomes the obstacles and ascends the hill to claim his bride.
full_text: Once on a time there was a princess who lived in a castle atop a hill made of glass. The hill was so smooth and slippery that no one could climb it; all who tried simply slid back down. The king declared that whoever could ascend the glass hill and reach the princess would be allowed to marry her. Many brave knights and strong men tried, but all failed. Then came a young man of humble origin but great heart. He encountered a series of magical creatures who, impressed by his kindness, gave him a magical ointment to rub on his shoes, allowing him to grip the glass. With great effort and determination, he climbed the steep, transparent slope. Upon reaching the summit, he found the princess, and having proved his worth and courage, they were wed and lived in happiness.
moral: Courage, determination, and kindness can overcome the most impossible of obstacles.

title: The Prince and the Princess in the Forest
summary: A romantic tale of two lovers separated by tragic circumstances and reunited through magic and enduring love in a mystical forest setting.
full_text: In the rich tradition of Danish folklore, the tale of The Prince and the Princess in the Forest is told. A romantic tale of two lovers separated by tragic circumstances and reunited through magic and enduring love in a mystical forest setting. This story has been passed down through generations, reflecting the values and fears of the people of Denmark, weaving together the mundane and the magical in a way that captures the essence of the Nordic spirit.
moral: True love transcends all boundaries.

summary: A prince is cursed to live as a white bear. He is eventually saved and restored to human form through the unwavering love and devotion of a princess.
